sentences.text: We knew it was Mengele.
Because we heard the name from the Hungarian girls that came to the camp, the Hungarian Jews told us about it.
He was a very good-looking man.
And we, each one of-- and also some of the policeman, the Jewish policemen mentioned to us that we are-- that this is Mengele.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we had to get undressed, strip, undress, and carry our belongings.
At which point I removed my glasses also.
I was uncertain if its-- I'm doing the right thing.
Because I could have fallen on one of the Germans.
But I did that.
Why?
Automatically I did that.
As I was getting undressed, I automatically removed my glasses, and I never put them on.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And he did a selection on a few people, he-- elderly people, elderly women, elderly men.
Plaszow still had some children, which were-- either paid-- with that the parents paid off to bring them, whichever way they were able to.
I don't deny them the-- the right to do what was right at that time.
And if they had a chance to save them, why not?
After the selection, the names-- some of the Nazis were sitting and taking the numbers-- we had in Plaszow numbers, but I do not remember mine.
But there were-- whoever was put aside, went to the right, had to give their name.
Plaszow still had their paper with the number on it.
And they had to register there.
And we passed by.
And to get dressed, we tried to go into the latrine to dress.
And we heard little voices.
Don't do anything-- don't do-- please, don't do it.
We are here.
We are here.
Some of the children hid in the holes.
It was a horrible, petrifying [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sight.
It was a petrifying idea.
But it was a bright idea.
Some of those children that hid there are living now.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that was-- that's when I met Dr.
Mengele in person.
The second time I met him upon arrival to Auschwitz-- there was--
Yeah.
Because the next Sunday--
The next Sunday trucks pulled up, and these people had to go.
They had the amount of people.
They knew how many, and they had to go.
And there was no question about it.
Because the police and the Nazis knew who to look for, who to search for.
And they knew which barrack they were in.
So therefore they had to go.
They were taken away on a transport to Auschwitz.
They did not survive.
I guess they went to the oven right away.
But this is what happened to that group of-- I don't know how many hundreds of people were taken at that time.
And I remained in Plaszow.
Again, we settled into our daily routine.
And on August 6-- 5th or 6th-- I don't-- I know that I arrived to Auschwitz on August 6th that I went into-- so I'm trying to figure out.
It was a daytime shift-- night shift-- and we did not go to our barracks.
That was on the 6th that straight from the work, whatever it was, we went onto the trains.
And we tried not to be in the front.
We tried to go back, and back, and back.
But they had room for 7,000 people.
And 7,000 had to go on that train.
The cattle trains. 7,000 of young, able-bodied people, men and women, were taken to Auschwitz.
We arrived toward evening.
It was hot.
It was humid.
It was miserable in those trains.
But we got out.
Till this day-- till this day, I cannot understand it-- I don't know.
I know the German efficiency.
But this was beyond me, to conduct the selection, to conduct who goes to the oven in clothes, and to separate the men from women, and it was in five minutes the platform was clean and clear.
It was in five minutes.
Till this day, I don't understand how it all happened.
But it happened.
I was led this way, to the left, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to an island between two buildings.
We smelled the flesh, the burned flesh.
We saw the chimneys, and we realized that those are the ovens, that we actually are standing in front of it.
And again, we had to get undressed on that lawn, on that little lawn, women.
Men where somewheres else.
We had to get undressed there.
Put down our clothes and make sure you know where your clothes are left, so you can pick it up later, after the selection.
And the Sonderkommando was walking around.
And a woman came over to me, and says, you are from Cracow.
You are so and so.
I said, yes.
Your sister was Pola.
I said, yes.
By Mengele remove your glasses.
And I had, and I took off my glasses.
I put them in the palm of my hand.
And I walked straight with my hand down, and I passed Mengele.
I went to the left.
Other people, well, some people went to the right.
The selection took place, was in a half hour, we were-- whatever group was left there, whether 1,000 or whether 2,000, I have no idea how many of us were left there sitting there.
And we had to wait our turn.
Meanwhile, night came on.
And the flame, not from the chimney, but the flame nearby-- later on I realized what it was.
They were over-- working overtime.
The ovens could not burn that many bodies.
So some of the bodies were burnt in the pit nearby.
And that flame from that pit made the night-- I can't say daytime-- I never saw light like this.
But illuminated the whole area.
We did not need the reflecting lights that the guards were throwing at us.
They could very well see that we were sitting there.
And then the time came.
And we had to go in single file into that room.
And we were shaved, every part of our bodies was shaved.
And we were led into showers.
And we were given a piece of soap with RIF on it, Reichs Judan Fett, which means pure Jewish fat.
And it was a soft piece, a square little soft piece.
And I says, which part of my parents or my sisters am I going to be washed with.
That's what went through my mind.
And we got in there, and a little water dripped down.
Nobody recognized anybody.
We didn't know who was where.
But I recognized a cousin of mine, because she had a little growth near her right ear.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went over quiet, and I says [NON-ENGLISH].
And here she recognized my name.
And we stood, supposedly under the shower.
And we waited, and a few drops of water came down.
And we hear a German announce, a woman announcement, [GERMAN].
That means, soap yourself.
And I said to my cousin, I says, tell me.
Does the gas work better when your body is wet?
What are they doing?
And we're waiting.
And then a little more water comes down.
[GERMAN], rinse yourself off.
And if you had leather shoes, you had to leave them behind.
If you had wooden shoes, you were able to have them with you.
And I had a pair of wooden shoes.
So I had to dunk them in a-- in a little basin there that was built in in the floor.
That means, if anything was hidden in them, it would show up, I guess.
And they let us outside.
And I just couldn't believe that we were not gassed.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they gave us clothes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You see, the Hungarian girls came at least in those-- not uniforms, but those gray dresses.
At least it was something that you put on you.
I was handed a dress, a silk dress, a good-looking dress.
But the skirt was cut up like a hula-- like a hula skirt.
That when you walked you see everything.
That was my clothes that I was given, no underwear, no nothing.
Nobody was given any underwear.
And we had to take a drink of coffee.
Till this day, I feel that bitterness in my mouth.
The SS people were standing over us with whips.
We had to drink the full cup.
It was water with some leaves in it.
Till this day, I don't know what it was.
But it was-- I guess my daughter tried to find out.
And she was told, most likely some sort of a hormone.
Because the following day each one of us, whoever was on that transport and drank that, quote, unquote, 'coffee,' got her period.
Each one of us.
And the blood was running through the legs.
There was no water to wash.
There was nothing to cover yourself with.
There was nothing to help yourself with.
You just had-- so nature had to take its course the way you were.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we were led outside of that area onto the road, and the orchestra was playing Mendelssohn's March.
Perfect music, perfect musicians, and we had to walk in that gooey soil.
Because Poland has very rich soil, and it's full of gook after a rain, after snow.
It's gooey.
And we had to walk to the tap of the music of that march.
That music I'll never forget as long as I live.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were led into the 2B2.
That was, for the next five months, that was my address.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] First I was in block 9.
It was a Jewish blockalteste.
She was a holy terror.
She was a holy, holy, terror.
I've never seen anything like it.
I don't think that I ever met a human being that should be so miserable, rotten, unhappy.
Yes, each one of us lost families.
But she was a holy terror, especially-- I'll use a Hungarian expression, [HUNGARIAN], that's a Polish girl.
She was Hungarian.
And the world was guilty of everything that happened to her.
And she was just like a wild animal let out of the cage.
She did not have the whip, but she had a stick in her hand.
And we tried very hard not to be within her reach ever, ever.
Because whoever was within her reach, it was just amazing how she did not break bones.
That the rage in her was incredible.
All right.
We were 13 on a bunk area, which had only boards.
And 13 of us were sleeping on that.
The shoes that we had were our pillow.
And we slept feet to feet.
That's one night that we had some of our heads were on the wall.
Some of the heads were on the outside.
And we slept.
And if one of us had to turn, all 13 had to move.
Because otherwise there was no room.
How none of us ever got splinters on our bodies, I'll never realize-- I'll never know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But we consoled our-- we were consoling ourselves.
Maybe tomorrow will get better.
Maybe one day it will be better.
We were very hungry, because the bread, that pound of bread was cut up in eight portions.
Each one of us had a little bit like this.
That was the portion for the day, and some soup.
And if you were lucky that you had the bowl, you were able to sit down and eat it.
If you were lucky that somebody handed you that bowl.
You couldn't even wash it.
Because it was no place to wash it.
So whatever somebody else had, you had it too.
But you didn't think about it.
Because you were hungry.
So you ate.
And we were looking forward to that soup.
Because it was one and only meal for the day.
I'll never forget.
There was a transport brought in from Greece.
Afterwards I found that it was from the island of Rhodes.
Those were Greek Jews who escaped Greece.
And we watched those women in the Panama-- Panama hats, beautiful white hats, and white suits.
And the men, and beautiful suits, white suits.
A handful of them was led into B2B, and to that compound, a handful of women and a handful of men to the A compound.
I'll never forget that morning.
I was handed the soup about 10 o'clock in the morning.
And I couldn't wait.
I was so hungry from the night before, from the evening before.
I wanted it, so I found myself a little spot in the back of the barrack.
I sat on the floor and I was eating with my hands, because there was no spoon.
If you were lucky, you got a spoon, you were lucky.
But that day I didn't have any.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one of the Greek women is screaming across to the men, where is her husband, and where is her husband.
I don't speak Greek.
I couldn't understand.
But then she turns around to me and with such anger starts yelling at me in French.
[SPEAKING FRENCH]
How in the world, she says to me, how can you eat when my husband is not there?
He is dead.
And you're eating?
And I must have had a very, pitiful expression on my face.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because I just looked at her.
I couldn't even answer her.
I feel-- I felt very bad for her.
Because it was her first tragedy.
And I just looked at her.
She turned around and walked away.
I don't think I ever saw her again.
But it was so pathetic.
That's why it just like remained with me, that incident.
There were many more, but this stayed with me badly.
I saw a woman being killed by a Nazi on his-- on-- riding his bicycle across her neck.
I saw dogs eating up people, getting for the jugular.
Once a Dutch woman was missing, somebody was missing from block 27.
And we were standing outside in the rain and drizzle for hours, endless, until about 11 o'clock at night, until they found a dead body on the wires, on the electrical wires.
And then again, we had to stay there.
Because they had to shut off the electricity to remove the body, so nobody else will be electrocuted.
But then we were told we had to stay.
Because you have no right to kill yourself.
See?
The Jewish bodies were very important for nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This blockalteste that you talked about, the Hungarian woman who was so brutal--
Yes?
I have no idea.
I have no idea.
Because afterwards I was moved to a different block, to a different barrack, to barrack 27.
Oh, yes.
You want to see it?
My number is A19165.
Can you pick it up?
OK.
And during my stay in Auschwitz, the first few days, a young man came with a group of latrine workers.
That means they had to clean it out on daily basis.
And all of a sudden he came in, he recognized me.
He went over to me.
And he says to me, what happened to my sister?
And I said, she was separated.
I says, what do you mean what happened to your sister?
Wasn't she with you?
And he says-- he says to me, yes, we were on the train.
They were born in Katowice.
And they recognized the countryside.
And he jumped out of the train.
And a few other guys jumped.
Some of the guys were killed.
He and another guy were brought into Auschwitz.
And they got a punishment bunker to stay in a 2 by 2 for six months.
The other guy did not survive.
He survived that ordeal.
And at that time, he was brought in September '43, this was already '44.

January '44 there was an edict given, a law passed that whoever is in Auschwitz for a longer time, whoever was alive in Auschwitz at that time, these people would not undergo a selection unless they're sick.
And being he was in that bunker, he survived.
He was let out of that bunker, and led into the D camp for men.
And he-- he was living.
And so he came to see who that he knew is alive.
Very few people remained there.
His sister went to the oven.
And he told me.
My sister went.
That transport went straight to the oven.
And his sister didn't survive either.
She was not with me anymore.
And those were the little incidents.
There was another blockalteste who came to my cruel blockalteste and asked directly for me one morning to take me to work.
And she was very reluctant to allow that to happen.
But that other one, I don't know what she said, whatever, I did not know anything.
But all of a sudden, I hear my name being called.
And as a matter of fact, that one that requested my presence with her, she said to the blockalteste, in my presence.
You keep her food.
Because I'll bring her back.
And she has to eat.
And she took me across the road out of the B2B, across the road, to what had used to be gypsy camp.
And with other girls, we carried pails, and we had to put in sand into the bell, and bring it to our camp.
That they needed the sand for something there.
And after we got across to that gypsy compound, she took me by my hand and she says, I have a surprise for you.
My cousin was there, a man, whom she knew.
They grew up, more or less, in the same little town there.
And she knew of him and he knew of her.
And via grapevine, my cousin discovered that I am there.
So she gave us five minutes together.
And a couple weeks later, she came to look for me, and to let me know that he was taken on a transport, where she did not know.
But this is what-- there were some human people in all that too.
Not everybody was ugly.
Not everybody was mean.
And not everybody was nasty.
There were some humane people.
We're going to stop for a moment to change the tape.
